title: Can AI actually help reduce trading bias — or does it just add another layer of noise?
author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
selftext:
Where do One thing I find interesting with trading is that the problem isn’t always strategy — it’s decision consistency.

Even with a defined system, traders still:

* override signals
* interpret structure differently depending on bias
* take trades that don’t fully meet their own criteria

So I’ve been thinking about this:

Can AI realistically help reduce that layer of subjectivity?

Not by predicting the market, but by:

* enforcing consistent rules
* analyzing structure the same way every time
* highlighting when a setup is unclear or low-conviction

In theory, it could act as a “decision filter” rather than a signal generator.

But in practice:  
👉 does it actually improve decision-making?  
👉 or just add another opinion to ignore?

Curious to hear from people here working with trading systems or AI:

Where do you see real value — and where does it fall short?you see real value — and where does it fall short?

comments.author: No-Consequence-1779
comments.body:
LLMs are non deterministic. They produce a different answer for the same question. The answers are based upon probability.  

Use math for rules. If you’re having trouble doing that, an LLM will just ad more unpredictability and latency.

replies.author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
replies.body:
That’s a fair point — and I agree LLMs aren’t deterministic in the same way rule-based systems are.
But I think it depends on how you use them.
If you rely on them to generate signals directly, then yes, you’re introducing variability and noise.
But if you use them more as a structured analysis layer, the goal isn’t strict determinism — it’s consistency in how a setup is evaluated.
Also, with proper constraints (prompt design, temperature control, predefined logic), you can reduce a lot of that variability.
I see it less as replacing math-based rules, and more as complementing them — especially in areas where interpretation is usually subjective (like structure or context).
Curious how you see it — do you think there’s zero use case for LLMs in discretionary-style trading, or just not for execution?

comments.author: Jabba_au
comments.body:
1/ I built an AI that trades crypto for me 24/7.

It scans 70+ markets every 5 minutes, buys momentum breakouts, and manages exits automatically.

Here's exactly how it works 🧵

2/ The problem: I was watching charts all day, missing trades at 3am, and letting emotions override my strategy.

The fix: automate everything with rules-based execution.

3/ The strategy: Trend-Following Momentum

Every 5 min, the bot:
→ Scans all Binance USDT pairs
→ Filters by volume (>$5M)
→ Checks RSI, breakout level, volume spike
→ Only buys when ALL 4 signals align

From 200+ pairs → usually 0-3 candidates.

4/ The exit system (where the money is made):

• -12% stop loss
• +30%: sell 20%, stop → breakeven
• +50%: sell 20%, trail 20%
• +100%: sell 20%, trail 10%
• 72h time exit if no movement

You lock in gains while letting winners run.

5/ Risk management:

• Kill switch at 50% portfolio drawdown
• Daily loss limit: 20%
• Max 2 positions at once
• Every stop is set on entry

The bot can't "revenge trade." It follows rules.

6/ The coolest part: adaptive learning.

Every 10 trades, the bot reviews its win rate.

Winning >60%? → Loosens filters, catches more trades
Winning <35%? → Tightens up, gets selective

It adapts to changing market conditions.

7/ Infrastructure:

• $6/mo VPS (DigitalOcean Sydney)
• Python + Binance API
• Telegram alerts for every trade
• Auto-restarts on crash

Total cost: a coffee per month.

8/ First day live:
• Bought TOWNS at $0.0046 (RSI 73, vol 7.5x)
• Bought MBOX at $0.0189 (RSI 64.8, vol 3.7x)

Both found and executed in under 2 seconds.

I was eating dinner.

9/ I documented the entire system — strategy, code, deployment — in a step-by-step guide.

No coding experience needed. Copy the bot, configure, deploy.

Full source code (644 lines) included.

→ myclawtrade.com

10/ If you're still manually watching charts in 2026, you're leaving money on the table.

Automate the boring parts. Let AI handle execution. Focus on strategy.

→ myclawtrade.com
